query: Why isn’t a UI element being announced or focused by the screen reader, and how can its accessibility labels help me figure out what’s going on?

reranked_docs.title: Google I/O 2025 – fKY6qYxytLI
reranked_docs.text:
Last year, we strongly
discouraged the use of accessibility
announcements, a concept that often overoptimizes the
experience for users of screen readers and may produce
inconsistent, sometimes disruptive experiences. With Android 16,
we're officially deprecating the APIs used to
generate these announcements. And we're planning to end
support for TalkBack's handling of them in a future release. To make it easier
to test for this, TalkBack's speech
output overlay, which you can enable from
its developer settings, will turn red when
producing feedback from accessibility
announcements. If your app relies
on TYPE_ANNOUNCEMENT AccessibilityEvents or view's
announceForAccessibility method, now is the time to migrate to
a more semantic alternative. These alternatives include
providing descriptive labels that clearly communicates
actions associated with UI elements,
eliminating the need to provide confirmation
announcements for most user interactions; using window
and pane titles to convey a high-level description for
activities and fragments, which are described by
TalkBack automatically during transitions; or defining
live regions on UI elements that appear temporarily or
update their descriptions if users should be made aware of
those related content changes. For more details,
check out the announce for accessibility API
documentation linked from the description below. Finally, an update on testing-- we've heard many
requests from developers for accessibility checks that
work from composed tests. And today I'm excited to
share that in Compose 1.8, we've released an integration
between Android's Accessibility Test Framework and the
Compose UI test library. This new offering can
leverage existing tests using Android Compose
test rule to identify some common accessibility
issues automatically. To enable these
checks, add the UI test JUnit 4 accessibility
dependency, call the enable accessibility
checks extension function on Android
Compose test rule, and trigger a test action, such
as calling perform click OK.
reranked_docs.speaker: unknown

reranked_docs.title: Google I/O 2025 – fKY6qYxytLI
reranked_docs.text:
We've added a new concept known
as a supplemental description, which offers a structured way to
add an additional accessibility label to UI elements in order
to describe their purpose. Supplemental descriptions
are particularly useful when defined on
containers or focusable groupings of UI. They should be
used in cases where you want to expose some
additional information about the group in a way
that won't override the text or description from its subtree. You can think of
supplemental descriptions as a complement to content
descriptions, which offer similar labeling
functionality but will override the descriptions
from the group's descendants. This API allows you to augment
how a screen reader would describe your UI without having
to build up your own content descriptions, which
can be error prone or introduce challenges
with localization. As an example, a
supplemental description might be used on
a custom dropdown menu that doesn't otherwise have
a label describing its purpose. In this case, we use font_family
to convey that information to users while preserving other
text labels that represent the currently selected font. We've also added XML
attributes and APIs on view to make it more
convenient to provide supplemental descriptions. The accessibility API now
offers a structured way to represent a UI element's
expanded or collapsed state. Previously, the
best approach was to surface this information
through the free-form state description property,
but we now recommend migrating to this new API. AccessibilityNodeInfo's
new expandedState property allows you to convey
whether something on screen is collapsed, partially,
or fully expanded. And the new expanded
content change type on AccessibilityEvent
signals any change in that UI's expanded state
to accessibility services. When UI can be expanded
and collapsed by users, you should continue
to support and respond to action expand and action
collapse accessibility actions, which are supported
going back to API 21. We've also added a
way for developers

reranked_docs.title: Google I/O 2025 – 80qkStdDWXQ
reranked_docs.text:
Accessibility
Services, you might run into unexpected
accessibility behaviors that are difficult to trace. But there's a good chance
that semantic properties can help you understand
why your components are behaving the way they are. You can debug with a
tool of your choice, from Layout Inspector
in Android Studio, to TreeDebug in TalkBack
developer settings, to composeTestRule.printToLog. These tools provide information
about nodes and their properties that are exposed to
accessibility services by Compose. As an example, let's take
a look at Layout Inspector. What if you had a screen
with three elements, but with accessibility
services on, the first one wasn't
being selected and the second one didn't have
any action feedback associated with it? We can examine the
semantic properties to see if there's anything suspicious. The component tree
in Layout Inspector contains information
about an element's bounds, parameters, and
semantic properties. In our tree, we see
all three elements, but the first one has a hide
from accessibility applied. That can help us debug why
it isn't being selected. Maybe the element is
marked as hidden somewhere in the semantics
tree, or Compose considers it obscured because
of some decorative overlay. The second element
has a focus property, but no on-click like
the previous element. So this element is probably
missing a clickable modifier somewhere, which is why
an accessibility service, like TalkBack, may not announce
an action signal to the user, like double tap to activate. If we look at the
third element, it has all the properties we want. It is focusable,
has an on-click, and other additional
semantics are applied, which is why it's
interpreted as expected. In this way, debugging
tools can help investigate why certain announcements
or selections aren't happening with your
accessibility services. Much of what we covered today
can be manually verified, for example, by turning
an accessibility service, like Switch Access,
on and checking that all works as expected.

In Android 16, we've
made an addition to AccessibilityNodeInfo's
Range Info class to offer a reliable way
to convey this state. When displaying such
a progress indicator, we recommend using the new
indeterminate RangeInfo type on the accessibility node
info emitted by that element. You can continue to set a
content description or state description too if you'd like
to convey additional information to users. Next up, I'd like to highlight
some recently updated accessibility best practices. As you heard from
Lisie, we're continuing to improve TalkBack's image
description features, which automatically offer detailed
information about images and photos. Developers play
an important role in ensuring this feature
works well across apps. We continue to
recommend providing your own descriptive and
localized accessibility labels throughout
your UIs. But in cases where that's not
possible, such as where images from user-generated
content are displayed, there's another important
step in ensuring these image description features work well. Conveying image semantics
from UI elements that may benefit from
additional descriptions will cue TalkBack to offer
or automatically generate image descriptions. When using Compose,
this is accomplished by using or extending
an image Composable. If you're working with views,
use an ImageView, ImageButton, or one of their subclasses. If you're using a
custom view, override getAccessibilityClassName and
return android.widget.ImageView. As we continue to evolve
Android's Accessibility API, we're focusing on offering
app developers the ability to convey a richer
set of UI semantics that generalize to a wider
range of assistive technologies. We're also moving away
from APIs and practices that are tied to the behaviors
of specific accessibility services. Last year, we strongly
discouraged the use of accessibility
announcements, a concept that often overoptimizes the
experience for users of screen readers and may produce
inconsistent, sometimes disruptive experiences.

reranked_docs.text:
When UI can be expanded
and collapsed by users, you should continue
to support and respond to action expand and action
collapse accessibility actions, which are supported
going back to API 21. We've also added a
way for developers to convey partially
checked controls within the accessibility API. You'll sometimes
encounter this pattern in hierarchical lists, where
a checkbox for a parent group uses this state to represent
that some of its children are selected while
others are not. We've deprecated
AccessibilityNodeInfo's Boolean checked property and replaced
it with an integer property that can represent true, false,
or partial checked states. We've also added a new
content change type on AccessibilityEvent to convey
a change in a UI element's checked state. Previously, sending a
TYPE_VIEW_CLICKED accessibility event from a checkable
element was expected. We still recommend dispatching
that event on earlier API versions, but you should begin
using this new approach starting at API 36 to ensure
screenreaders provide feedback when the checked state
of a control changes. Another addition to the
accessibility API in Android 16 relates to how apps represent
form fields that require user input or selection. A common practice is to indicate
required or optional fields using a visible text
label, sometimes relying on an asterisk to denote
which fields require input. In addition to
visible labels, we now also recommend using
AccessibilityNodeInfo's new fieldRequired property
to convey this information to Accessibility Services
in a structured way. Previously, there was not
a consistent way for apps to indicate to
Accessibility Services that a UI element was displaying
an indeterminate state, such as loading
indicators and progress bars without a numerical
progress value. In Android 16, we've
made an addition to AccessibilityNodeInfo's
Range Info class to offer a reliable way
to convey this state. When displaying such
a progress indicator, we recommend using the new
indeterminate RangeInfo

with manual verification, you should also use
automated testing to flag any potential issues that
could impact user experiences. Existing testing
APIs in Compose allow you to write
automated tests that interact with semantic
elements, and to assert properties defined in your UI. But you can now use the
Accessibility Test Framework, the same framework underlying
Accessibility Scanner and Accessibility
Checks in Espresso, to perform some
checks automatically, starting with Compose 1.8. To do so, add the UI test
junit4 accessibility library to use accessibility checks. To enable them, call
enable accessibility checks in the Android Compose test rule
and trigger an action, or try perform accessibility checks. This specific test fails with
an exception and a message that the item may not
have a label readable by accessibility services. Other checks can look for color
contrast, touch target size, or elements traversal order. In combination with
manual testing, automated tests that use

on in the development life cycle. We've covered a lot today,

reranked_docs.text:
To enable these
checks, add the UI test JUnit 4 accessibility
dependency, call the enable accessibility
checks extension function on Android
Compose test rule, and trigger a test action, such
as calling perform click OK. You can also invoke try
perform accessibility checks to explicitly run the
accessibility checks at any point during a test. If the accessibility
test framework identifies a potential
issue, it will throw an exception failing the test. You can customize the behavior
of the accessibility checks by providing your own
AccessibilityValidator instance. This allows you to do things
like configure the severity level for problems that will
cause tests to fail or provide a matcher for suppressing
failures from known issues. We continue to strongly
recommend developers test apps by using them with Android's
assistive technologies to best understand the
experience users will encounter. These automated checks
complement manual testing by accelerating the
detection of a variety of possible
accessibility barriers. We've covered a lot
here, from updates to accessibility features
related to vision, hearing, and dexterity to changes
in our APIs, best practices, and tooling. Be sure to check out the
links in the description to learn more
about these topics. Thanks for joining us today
and for doing your part to build an ecosystem of highly
accessible and inclusive apps. [MUSIC PLAYING]

and welcome to our session on how to build more accessible

Relations Engineer at Google. MELBA NUZEN: And I'm Melba
Nuzen, a Software Engineer working on Jetpack Compose. SIMONA MILANOVIC: Developing
with accessibility in mind means making your apps usable
by even more people, including those with accessibility
needs who may use Android devices in different ways. So, in this talk,
we'll focus on how to leverage powerful declarative
Compose APIs and tools to build apps that are more
inclusive and accessible. We'll cover practical
steps and concepts in supporting
accessibility with Compose, starting with how to classify
composables and modify them with the appropriate
APIs and semantics. We'll also show how to inspect
the composable semantics with tools that can
be used for debugging unexpected behaviors
when used with Android's assistive technologies. Finally, we'll cover how to
detect common accessibility issues and automate some
aspects of testing with Compose accessibility checks. By the end, you'll have the
right tools and techniques to create more
delightful and inclusive experiences for more users. For updates in

Foundation and UI APIs, and custom components. These range from none to
minimal semantic modifications for material to potentially
larger structural changes for more custom components. This classification helps
you choose and integrate more information into the UI
elements earlier in the app development. This information on

query: What do semantics mean in accessible user interfaces, and how do standard UI elements handle those semantics by default?

for material to potentially
larger structural changes for more custom components. This classification helps
you choose and integrate more information into the UI
elements earlier in the app development. This information on
the meaning and role of a component that is sent
to accessibility services is called semantics. For example, a camera icon
might be visually just an image, but the semantic meaning
could be take a photo. To learn more about
accessibility basics ahead of this talk, check out the
linked documentation and video. By combining the right
semantics with Compose APIs, you provide as much information
as possible about your component to accessibility services,
which then decide how and what to represent
to the user. MELBA NUZEN: Let's
look at each category, starting with
material components. These pre-built UI elements
include built-in semantics that follow their specific
role and function, meaning that most
accessibility support is provided for you with little
or no additional work. For example, a material
button might consist of a child icon and some text. Instead of treating the
children as individuals, a material button merges its
children's semantics by default so that accessibility services
can treat them as one entity. Merging is a powerful
concept in semantics and widely used,
as we'll see later. But in general,
material components require little
adjustment in order to be more accessible when used
for their intended purpose. Similarly, in Compose
UI and Foundation APIs, many accessible practices
are also followed by default. Using the right APIs
for the right purpose usually means you get
predefined semantics that cover standard use cases,
but you should double check that these
defaults fit your needs. If it isn't enough
for some cases, Compose also provides
the tools to adjust for more specific requirements. Most semantic
properties are a way to simply provide
additional information. Setting something like a
content description or a state

reranked_docs.title: Google I/O 2025 – 80qkStdDWXQ
reranked_docs.text:
Compose also provides
the tools to adjust for more specific requirements. Most semantic
properties are a way to simply provide
additional information. Setting something like a
content description or a state description provides more
metadata about a UI component to services that
consume semantics. But some semantics require
more careful consideration, since they can influence how
the structure of a screen is interpreted, rather
than only provide additional helpful information. Two, Compose APIs
are specifically meant to support
accessibility traversal order. When using composables
with predefined semantics, there could be a need to
modify their traversal order, or the order in which
accessibility services navigate through UI elements. By default, Compose arranges
elements in expected reading order, usually top to
bottom, then left to right. While this normally
creates a logical order, there are some scenarios where
top to bottom, left to right is not sufficient and Compose
might need additional hints. In these cases, you can use
Compose's isTraversalGroup and traversalIndex APIs. IsTraversalGroup marks
logical semantic groups that need customization,
while traversalIndex adjusts the order of individual
elements within those groups. Let's look closer at
the clock face example. The UI consists of
a circular layout where the numbers of
the clock are placed, and some clock text containing
the actual number values. To influence the
default traversal order, we set isTraversalGroup equals
true on the surrounding parent, in this case, the
circular layout. Then we assign increasing
traversalIndex values to each child
element so that they can be numbered numerically. To influence traversal
behavior, this API must be set on a
component that will be selectable by
accessibility services. In this case, we set the
index on the text itself rather than the
containing box parent. With those changes, we've
adjusted our traversal order to be more logical for
accessibility services.

We've added a new concept known
as a supplemental description, which offers a structured way to
add an additional accessibility label to UI elements in order
to describe their purpose. Supplemental descriptions
are particularly useful when defined on
containers or focusable groupings of UI. They should be
used in cases where you want to expose some
additional information about the group in a way
that won't override the text or description from its subtree. You can think of
supplemental descriptions as a complement to content
descriptions, which offer similar labeling
functionality but will override the descriptions
from the group's descendants. This API allows you to augment
how a screen reader would describe your UI without having
to build up your own content descriptions, which
can be error prone or introduce challenges
with localization. As an example, a
supplemental description might be used on
a custom dropdown menu that doesn't otherwise have
a label describing its purpose. In this case, we use font_family
to convey that information to users while preserving other
text labels that represent the currently selected font. We've also added XML
attributes and APIs on view to make it more
convenient to provide supplemental descriptions. The accessibility API now
offers a structured way to represent a UI element's
expanded or collapsed state. Previously, the

whether something on screen is collapsed, partially,

content change type on AccessibilityEvent

Last year, we strongly
discouraged the use of accessibility
announcements, a concept that often overoptimizes the
experience for users of screen readers and may produce
inconsistent, sometimes disruptive experiences. With Android 16,
we're officially deprecating the APIs used to
generate these announcements. And we're planning to end
support for TalkBack's handling of them in a future release. To make it easier
to test for this, TalkBack's speech
output overlay, which you can enable from
its developer settings, will turn red when
producing feedback from accessibility
announcements. If your app relies
on TYPE_ANNOUNCEMENT AccessibilityEvents or view's
announceForAccessibility method, now is the time to migrate to
a more semantic alternative. These alternatives include
providing descriptive labels that clearly communicates
actions associated with UI elements,
eliminating the need to provide confirmation
announcements for most user interactions; using window
and pane titles to convey a high-level description for
activities and fragments, which are described by
TalkBack automatically during transitions; or defining
live regions on UI elements that appear temporarily or
update their descriptions if users should be made aware of
those related content changes. For more details,
check out the announce for accessibility API
documentation linked from the description below. Finally, an update on testing-- we've heard many
requests from developers for accessibility checks that
work from composed tests. And today I'm excited to
share that in Compose 1.8, we've released an integration
between Android's Accessibility Test Framework and the
Compose UI test library. This new offering can
leverage existing tests using Android Compose
test rule to identify some common accessibility
issues automatically. To enable these
checks, add the UI test JUnit 4 accessibility
dependency, call the enable accessibility
checks extension function on Android
Compose test rule, and trigger a test action, such
as calling perform click OK.

reranked_docs.text: Hello everybody and welcome to this talk about unlocking ML performance with actionable diagnostics for TPUs and GPUs on Google cloud. My name is Reesh and I am the product management lead for diagnostics tooling for ML infrastructure software on Google cloud. Along with me I have my colleague Hira who is leading outbound product for developers on Google cloud. Today I'm very happy to announce a new cloud diagnostics profiling library as well as a newly updated XPRO tool. Both of these together enable a seamless profiling experience for ML engineers to performance optimize their ML workloads on any XLA based framework including Jax Pyrush XLA and car tensorflow on Google cloud. The cloud diagnostics xprof library has been built to enable a seamless self-hosted experience for Emma workflow profiling on GCP. The main features of this library include easy setup of infrastructure for xr using tensorboard as a host profile sharing to enable collaboration within user teams and with Google and ondemand profiling to capture profiles during your ML workload run. Check out this library on GitHub under AI hypercomputer cloud diagnostics XPRO. The core profiling tool XRO has also been newly updated. Previously you may be familiar with the tool TensorFlow profiler which we have now rebranded to XRP and move the codebase under the open XLA projects so we can support all XLA based frameworks used in the market with the same consistent experience. With this updated XPRO codebase and rebranding, we are now bringing the same tool used internally to optimize ML workloads within Google and Google DeepMine out to Google Cloud customers and the rest of the ecosystem. Let me now start by describing the features of the Cloud Diagnostics XPR library. To start out with, you need to set up your Google Cloud environment by setting up G-Cloud. Then all you need to do is pip install the latest version of the XPR library. In order to create a self-hosted tensorboard VM instance, you can use the XP profiler create command.

reranked_docs.title: Google I/O 2025 – ROFC-dQ421A
reranked_docs.text: Cloud environment by setting up G-Cloud. Then all you need to do is pip install the latest version of the XPR library. In order to create a self-hosted tensorboard VM instance, you can use the XP profiler create command. You will need to provide a zone and a GCS bucket path. Once a VM is created, you will get a link to access the VM and view profiles there if they are already captured in that directory. Alternatively, you can also connect to the VM using XP profiler connect and SSH into the VM. Once you have the VMs created, you can easily list and delete these VMs using XProfiler list and XProfiler delete. X-profiler list also prints the link name in case you missed clicking it previously during create. In order to capture profiles from your ML workload, you need to enable profile capture in your Jax, PyTorch or TensorFlow code. In all these frameworks, you enable profile capture with some type of start server. Now that you've enabled profile capture, you can capture your profiles either programmatically or on demand. Programmatically means you capture profiles beforehand in your code. With Jax, you use start trace and stop trace. For PyTorch, you use step trace or trace. And for TensorFlow you use trace. This will enable capture of profiles within your code when it runs. Alternatively, you can capture profiles on demand while your workload is running. This is done typically when you see some performance issue with your workload and want to collect profiles at that instant to diagnose issue. And in Chrome for a long time already, we have smaller expert models built in, for example, for translation and language detection, but also less visible features like notification prompt prediction to block spammy notification prompts and more. What's new now with built-in AI is that we've added a general purpose large language model or LLM for short. In the concrete case, it's actually a relatively small LLM called Gemini Nano. Unlike the other models, Gemini Nano doesn't ship with Chrome by default, but is downloaded dynamically on demand once a web app wants to make use of it. The reason for not shipping it by default is that while it's small for an LLM, it's still a larger resource. As a general purpose LLM, Geminano can be optimized for specific tasks like text summarization, creative writing and others. This happens through so-called fine-tunings. Combined the expert models, the German nano L&M and the finetunings are then exposed as highle APIs allowing the browser to match developer intent with the best model and fine-tuning for the task at hand. is creating user interfaces that

the different spatial UI components that

the concept of a subspace and how that relates to
spatial UI components. A subspace is a partition
of 3D space within your app, where you can build 3D spatial
layouts, place 3D models, or add depth to otherwise
two dimensional content. A subspace and its
contents are only rendered when
spatialization is enabled. That is, only when your app is
running on an XR enabled device and in Full Space mode. When your app is in
Home Space, or if it's running on a non-XR
enabled device, any code within that
subspace is ignored. A subspace can be
placed anywhere within your app's UI hierarchy. This can help you share things
like existing app architecture between XR and
other form factors, without needing to
re-architect your whole app. There are two categories
of spatial UI components in Android XR. Subspace composables,
which can only be rendered within a subspace,
and other spatialized components that do not have to be
called within a subspace. One of the most foundational
subspace composables is the spatial panel. You can think of a spatial
panel as a plane in 3D space, with a width and a height that

can be displayed. Spatial panels are the
fundamental building blocks of Android XR apps,
and they serve as containers for UI elements,
interactive components, and immersive content. The spatial panel

reranked_docs.text: vetted by Google? Can I trust them with my data? Yes. Before an add-on or an app is published to the marketplace, it undergoes two separate reviews by teams at Google. Oath verification focuses on making sure that an app doesn't misuse user data. And the app review focuses on the information that the app's developer provides in the Google Workspace marketplace as well as the functionality and usability of the app. As a developer, you can publish Google Workspace add-ons, editor add-ons, Google Chat apps, classroom add-ons, drive apps, and web apps to the Google Workspace marketplace. When publishing your solution to the marketplace, you can decide to make it available to your organization only or for anyone with a Google account. And one of these powerful integrations is between Gmail, Gemini, and Salesforce. Yes, that's an add-on that just recently launched and it brings your Salesforce data into Gmail with a super handy helper, Gemini. A great way to deploy a Google Workspace add-on is as an admin. You can install it for specific groups in your organization or your entire domain. Just as we mentioned, the admin will at this point be able to assess the add-on, check what type of data is accessed, and then install it. So, there's an extra layer of governance which many of our customers like having. Admins can choose to have full control by disabling users to install add-ons themselves and instead they can allow list add-ons as they see fit. Great. Now I have the ability to run kind of my own workspace app store with some of my own apps and some that I have approved specifically for my organization. But I often come across empowered teams that want to improve their workflows without involving the IT team. Where does this land on our map? That's where App Sheet comes in handy. query: What does "supplemental description" mean in accessibility, and how does it connect to the content descriptions used for Android UI elements?

reranked_docs.title: Google I/O 2025 – fKY6qYxytLI
reranked_docs.text:
We've added a new concept known
as a supplemental description, which offers a structured way to
add an additional accessibility label to UI elements in order
to describe their purpose. Supplemental descriptions
are particularly useful when defined on
containers or focusable groupings of UI. They should be
used in cases where you want to expose some
additional information about the group in a way
that won't override the text or description from its subtree. You can think of
supplemental descriptions as a complement to content
descriptions, which offer similar labeling
functionality but will override the descriptions
from the group's descendants. This API allows you to augment
how a screen reader would describe your UI without having
to build up your own content descriptions, which
can be error prone or introduce challenges
with localization. As an example, a
supplemental description might be used on
a custom dropdown menu that doesn't otherwise have
a label describing its purpose. In this case, we use font_family
to convey that information to users while preserving other
text labels that represent the currently selected font. We've also added XML
attributes and APIs on view to make it more
convenient to provide supplemental descriptions. The accessibility API now
offers a structured way to represent a UI element's
expanded or collapsed state. Previously, the
best approach was to surface this information
through the free-form state description property,
but we now recommend migrating to this new API. AccessibilityNodeInfo's
new expandedState property allows you to convey
whether something on screen is collapsed, partially,
or fully expanded. And the new expanded
content change type on AccessibilityEvent
signals any change in that UI's expanded state
to accessibility services. When UI can be expanded
and collapsed by users, you should continue
to support and respond to action expand and action
collapse accessibility actions, which are supported
going back to API 21. We've also added a
way for developers

Range Info class to offer a reliable way

a progress indicator, we recommend using the new

info emitted by that element. You can continue to set a
content description or state description too if you'd like

some recently updated accessibility best practices. As you heard from
Lisie, we're continuing to improve TalkBack's image
description features, which automatically offer detailed
information about images and photos. Developers play
an important role in ensuring this feature

your UIs. But in cases where that's not

from UI elements that may benefit from
additional descriptions will cue TalkBack to offer
or automatically generate image descriptions. When using Compose,
this is accomplished by using or extending
an image Composable. If you're working with views,
use an ImageView, ImageButton, or one of their subclasses. If you're using a
custom view, override getAccessibilityClassName and

set of UI semantics that generalize to a wider

from APIs and practices that are tied to the behaviors
of specific accessibility services. Last year, we strongly
discouraged the use of accessibility
announcements, a concept that often overoptimizes the
experience for users of screen readers and may produce
inconsistent, sometimes disruptive experiences.

or in a group chat, they might

Storage Access API works across
devices and latest browser versions. Another API that can support the
functionality of your solution without third-party cookies is
Federated Credential Management, or FedCM. With FedCM, users can sign in on
sites using account information and credentials from
identity providers, companies that have deep expertise
in user identity. FedCM lets you customize the
user identity experience. For example, you can choose
between different UI modes or configure the
user prompt to state exactly what user
data is requested. Additionally, an
active FedCM session serves as a trust signal
for the Storage Access API. That means that when
storage access is requested, your widget will
automatically get access to its top-level
unpartitioned storage. This accounts for a more
seamless user experience. No additional user
prompt is required. FedCM is a great option for
identity payments and content providers. FedCM is not
baseline, because it's a new feature that is
not yet supported by some of the widely used browsers. However, other
browsers are currently actively implementing FedCM. We've covered a lot

reranked_docs.text: more compliant, secure, and private AI into their products and services. Concerns we're hearing from customers are often around ensuring that AI systems are acting as intended based on the audience in question. For example, you might want to ensure that your application is only discussing topics relevant to children. Or you might want to help ensure that your product isn't creating posts with potentially offensive content. AI adversarial testing is our answer to application developers looking to test their generative AI implementations to ensure they're safe for users. This system replaces manual red teamming efforts performed by organizations today with an automated system. This system generates a wide range of adversarial prompts using an unfiltered model. The tool then takes these prompts and feeds them to your LLM, capturing any responses it receives. The response are then evaluated based on the content policies and a report is drafted that can be downloaded and shared with your team. This falls into a wider group of technical mitigations which best-in-class teams are deploying in order to ensure risk mitigation at every stage of generative AI integration and deployment. This also fits within Google's wider set of tooling to help developers in this area. In particular, once users receive their AI adversarial testing report, they can share it with their team and go to model armor in order to take advantage of capabilities such as AI guardrails, prompt injection protection, jailbreak protection, and more. We can draw an analogy here between AI guardrails, and a network firewall. Adversarial testing is a way to look for potential issues, and model armor lets you protect your systems in production. We're excited to put a tool in your hands so that you can work alongside your security counterparts to test AI implementations and take action at a deeper level of infrastructure via model armor. You can start by triggering the AI adversarial testing agent within Gemini Code Assist or Firebase Studio. The agent will respond by

And even with our
first attempt, what we were surprised was
we got to good accuracy not only of typical weather
conditions like here in California, which honestly
isn't that hard to predict, but extreme weather events-- storms, hurricanes, typhoons. Because you don't think as
a machine learning scientist that you would get that
because they're rare events. In the last four
decades, the dataset maybe has a handful of these. But again, the reason why
I believe this was possible is because there are
clear physical signatures. Hurricanes have this hot and
air cool mixing together. So it is not just the amount
of data, it's the signatures and how significant
are these events. And the model is
able to pick that up and is able to even do now
better than traditional models. And I think that took a lot of
weather scientists by surprise. But I'm also very heartened
to see how much adoption has happened at lightning speed. Because ECMWF made
all these models. And our model and
Google DeepMind has done excellent work after
that with GraphCast and others. They're available. It built its own model. It declared them as operational,
which is the gold standard, meaning side by side,
it's running today with traditional models. And weather scientists
can make judgments. And on the other key is
because of the speed, we can now build much
larger statistical ensembles of events like hurricanes. Because what you care about
is not just one prediction of where the hurricane
is going to go, because there's a
lot of uncertainty. You look at perturbations
of the current conditions and see how many
of those tracks are going to land in Florida,
for instance, in a very specific region. So each city can then decide
should the evacuation happen. If it's 90% versus 60% chance,
it's a very different policy decision. But now we can enable
that which was not possible with
traditional models. So it's not just, OK, accuracy
is somewhat better and faster, but now we can go to
the tail events, which is the hardest aspect.

query: What exactly is a qubit, and how does being in multiple states at once let a quantum computer scale exponentially?

You can think of it as a
light switch that's either in the on or the off position. But qubits use quantum
bits or qubits. And qubits can be in what are
called superposition states, 0 and 1 at the same time. And they have this
exponential scaling behavior. So every time you add
a qubit to your system, you can think of that as
doubling the computational power of your quantum computer. Fundamentally,
quantum computers are able to run on
quantum mechanics. And quantum mechanics is what
underlies all the physical laws of the world. So quantum computers,
in some sense, speak the natural
language of the universe. SINEAD BOVELL: OK. And we're going to get into
that a little bit more. So would you consider
quantum computing then to be the next big thing after
AI or is it more nuanced? Will quantum and AI
converge in some way and end up reinforcing
each other? JULIAN KELLY: Yes. It's a great question. I think it's a good mental
model that quantum computing is the next big thing after AI. It's distinct from it. But at the same time, they
do have the opportunity to complement each other. You can maybe think
of that in two ways. AI helps us with
our day to day jobs and building a quantum
computer right now. But also a quantum computer
has the opportunity to enhance AI systems as well. SINEAD BOVELL: And we're
going to get into some of the challenges with quantum. But I want to talk about
some of the possibilities. So why should we all
be paying attention to what's happening in quantum? What could theoretically
become possible in a world with quantum computers? JULIAN KELLY: Yeah. So there are a number
of application areas that we've identified that
quantum could be beneficial in. So for example, a
quantum computer could be critical in the
drug discovery process. So for example, we have a
paper from a few years ago where we worked on
this particular enzyme. It's called cytochrome
P450 enzyme. And you could imagine if
you're a drug maker, one of the things you do is you make
all these different candidate drugs.

We recently introduced new

physical keyboards. Mouse keys allows users to
control the on-screen mouse pointer using the keypad buttons
instead of a mouse or trackpad. This feature can be useful for
users with limited hand or arm movement, who may find
it difficult to use a traditional mouse. The Sticky Keys feature allows
you to press shortcut keys one at a time instead of having
to press multiple buttons simultaneously. Slow Keys allows you to adjust
how long you need to hold down a key before it's registered. And finally, Bounce Keys
ignores quickly repeated presses of the same key. All these features aim to
improve typing and navigation for users with

augmented generation to

easy-to-use abstractions for working with models
and vector stores. And this enables

regular functions, which means you can do
anything that you would also be able to do in
any other function, like calling other APIs,
retrieving data from a database, performing
computations, et cetera. But the cool thing is that
Genkit flows are observable. And that means you can monitor
them using the AI Monitoring dashboard in the
Firebase console or any other tool that supports
OpenTelemetry, such as Google Cloud's Observability Suite. Genkit also provides
easy-to-use abstractions for working with models
and vector stores. And this enables
us to tap into any of the recipes our users have
stored in their app's database and use them to generate new
recipes based on the ingredients the user has on hand. Another really cool
feature of Genkit is its unified model interface. This allows you to
use different models by just providing the
name of the model you want to use instead.

around game progression, leveling up characters or bases,

earned in three states, earned, unearned,

to market, and

KMPify everything? Well, hang on, not so fast. Jetpack has over 800 modules,
and not every library makes sense in a KMP world. So we started by clearly
defining our levels of support for each platform
that KMP supports. We classify the platforms
into three tiers. Our main focus is
mobile development. So our highest level of support
is for Android, iOS, and JVM targets. This allows you
to share code that uses Jetpack libraries
across your mobile apps and your Kotlin backend. Tier 2 is currently desktop
support for Mac and Linux. Tier 3, that's all the
other KMP platforms. The levels of support in
each tier are as follows. Tier 1, our highest
level of support-- it's fully tested
in CI and comes with strict binary
compatibility guarantees. Tier 2, it comes
with limited testing with host-side tests but no
binary compatibility guarantees. And tier 3, we ensure we don't
use any APIs during development that would block usage
on those platforms, but we provide no support. As well as the platforms,
we classify the libraries into the types of features
and guarantees we provide. Stable libraries bring both
binary compatibility and easy integration on
tier one platforms. Commonized libraries
mean the API is stable, so you get the binary
compatibility guarantees, but we aren't planning to
build platform-specific hooks. You might have to do some
manual integration yourself. And alpha, alpha hasn't changed
when it comes to Jetpack. The API is under
development, and the features are subject to change. With that, we've been
moving slow and steady, adding support for KMP to
multiple Jetpack libraries where we think it makes sense. We first started with Room,
DataStore, and Collections, which are now available
in a stable release. We've also commonized
Paging, and we've released alpha versions of
Saved State and View Model. Our KMP support isn't just
limited to Jetpack libraries, though.

standards. We all implement

Publishing for consumption
by other KMP projects through Gradle can be configured

a separate artifact for each target, with

to the library's name. A root publication
is also created, which includes

platform-specific artifact. Very similar to a

can directly integrate Gradle into Xcode by adding a Run
Script into your project's build phases. The Kotlin Gradle plugin

XCFramework and imports it into your Xcode project
when you run it this way. While this method is great for
quickly experimenting locally, it's really not the
recommended approach. In the real world,
many teams will have dedicated
iOS engineers that shouldn't need to interact
with Gradle or Kotlin directly. In this case, you'll
want to make your KMP library available
to Xcode projects through a package manager. We'll walk through two of the
most popular ones, Swift Package Manager and CocoaPods. Swift Package Manager is Apple's
officially supported method of distributing binary
packages, such as XCFrameworks for Xcode projects. For a new project,

modern best practices. And a Swift package
for a KMP library can be built directly
with official tooling. To publish a KMP library
as a Swift package, you'll need to assemble the
XCFramework through Gradle with the task shown here.

On the other hand, this
means capabilities may vary and customizations will differ. Though most browsers
have some level of support for Custom tabs,
it's important to verify the availability of
features and ensure compatibility of your
implementation across browsers. Here's how you would check
if the user's default browser supports Custom tabs,
with the get package name helper method. If no package is
returned, this means that Custom tabs
are not supported by the default browser. In this scenario,
you can continue to check if other browsers on
the device support custom tabs or fall back to a standard
action view intent to launch the URL in
any available browser. In general, Custom tabs
are good as a powerful, out-of-the-box
browsing experience. They allow you as
an app developer to develop your
primary app experience and also benefit from the
capabilities of full browsers while keeping users
within your app context. It is a simple and easy
way to provide your users a seamless transition to and
from an embedded web browsing experience. Thanks to the shared state. There's also reduced
friction for users if they've already signed
into and granted permissions to the site before. More importantly, the
user's credentials are protected and stay isolated
to any third-party sites. Keeping in mind the strengths
and limitations of Custom tabs, let's take a look
at some examples. In this example, you
can see that clicking on a link in a YouTube
video description opens up a Custom tab
displaying the website. In this use case, the link
is not controlled by YouTube, because the video uploader
manages the links that they'd like to show in the
description of their video, and the link goes to third-party
content not owned by YouTube. By using a Custom tab to
open these description links, the YouTube app doesn't have
to worry about the best way to integrate it into
its existing native app UI and experience. Instead, it can hand it off to
the device's default browser to provide the

Concerns we're hearing from customers are often around ensuring that AI systems are acting as intended based on the audience in question. For example, you might want to ensure that your application is only discussing topics relevant to children. Or you might want to help ensure that your product isn't creating posts with potentially offensive content. AI adversarial testing is our answer to application developers looking to test their generative AI implementations to ensure they're safe for users. This system replaces manual red teamming efforts performed by organizations today with an automated system. This system generates a wide range of adversarial prompts using an unfiltered model. The tool then takes these prompts and feeds them to your LLM, capturing any responses it receives. The response are then evaluated based on the content policies and a report is drafted that can be downloaded and shared with your team. This falls into a wider group of technical mitigations which best-in-class teams are deploying in order to ensure risk mitigation at every stage of generative AI integration and deployment. This also fits within Google's wider set of tooling to help developers in this area. In particular, once users receive their AI adversarial testing report, they can share it with their team and go to model armor in order to take advantage of capabilities such as AI guardrails, prompt injection protection, jailbreak protection, and more. We can draw an analogy here between AI guardrails, and a network firewall. Adversarial testing is a way to look for potential issues, and model armor lets you protect your systems in production. We're excited to put a tool in your hands so that you can work alongside your security counterparts to test AI implementations and take action at a deeper level of infrastructure via model armor. from generating stroke
shapes with stylus input, to rendering those strokes
quickly and efficiently, and even performing complex
geometric calculations for things like
erasing and selection. We built the ink Jetpack
library to simplify the process of creating,
rendering, and manipulating beautiful ink strokes. It provides a powerful
and modular toolkit for integrating rich inking
features into your app. The library consists
of six modules. The Strokes module is the
foundation of the ink library, handling core data types for
interacting with ink input. The Authoring module
manages the capture of user input and real
time rendering of strokes and facilitates
low-latency rendering. The Brush module manages
the visual style of strokes through properties like
color, size, and texture. The Geometry module provides
geometric primitives for shape manipulation
and enables features like stroke erasing
and selection. The Rendering
module incorporates canvasStrokeRenderer and
viewStrokeRenderer, offering performant rendering pipelines
with anti-aliasing support for Compose and
view-based UIs. Finally, the Storage module
provides efficient storage of stroke input data. As a developer, you can
use only the modules needed for your app's architecture. We've received great feedback
from our early partners. Ink API significantly reduced
the workload required for them to implement responsive
fluid inking in their app. Now let's take a look
at the Brush module and some of the
recent improvements we made with custom brushes. You can use the Brush module
in its factory methods to get started quickly
using stock brushes for pressure-sensitive
pins, highlighters, markers, and dashed lines. Here you see how I quickly
created a brush with the stock pressure pin and set the color,
size, and epsilon of the brush. We now have added support
for customized brushes with new experimental
APIs, which lets you unleash your
creativity to implement fun, expressive brushes,
or just match your existing set of brushes.

reranked_docs.text:
Then another new modifier, this
time to do visibility tracking. In the latest stable
version of Compose, we added onLayoutRectChanged. This is a low-level
API that you can use to track a composable's
position within either its roots, the
window, or the screen. And it unlocks various
use cases, mostly related to visibility tracking
or position tracking. Now, it's similar to
onGloballyPositioned, which you might already be
using to track the location. But this one is more
performance out of the box. And it allows you to add
throttle or debouncing, which makes it OK to use for
use cases like tracking the location of an item while
you're scrolling a lazy list. And in the alpha
versions of Compose, we've added some
higher-level APIs on top of this low-level
API, which are very, very specific for various use cases. So first of all, we added
onVisibilityChanged, which gives you a callback any
time the visibility of that composable changes, so it
enters or it exits the viewport. This is great for use cases like
automatically playing or pausing a video when it comes into view. And we're actually
using it in Androidify. We've also added
onFirstVisible, which is great for use cases
like impression tracking because it gives a callback
any time a composable has been in view for a
certain amount of time. So you can already start using
this stable onLayoutRectChanged modifier in your stable
version of Compose. And then you can use
these higher-level APIs when you use the alpha version. You can learn more
about these APIs and many more in
the two brand-new technical talks that we're
releasing as part of this year's I/O for best practices
in text input and best practices
in accessibility. But we haven't
been focusing only on adding new
features to Compose. And new features also shouldn't
be the only reason why you're upgrading your Compose version. Another consideration
is performance. When you run your app on
older versions of Compose, you might sometimes
see some jank. This is what some of
you have mentioned.

As many of you know,

query: What performance tweaks and release practices does Google use to keep their UI toolkit stable in production apps, like pausing parts of the UI, moving text measurement elsewhere, and prefetching items in long lists?

reranked_docs.title: Google I/O 2025 – 89UusPuz8q4
reranked_docs.text:
What you're used to is that
whenever we have a Compose change, we merge this into
the Compose main branch, and then it goes through
this alpha, beta, RC, and stable release cycle. But for Google
apps, this actually works a little bit differently
because all of the Google apps share a single version
of Compose among them. And we used to update that
version about once a month. But recently, we've
been working to update that about once every day. So any change in Compose that
makes it into the main branch, actually makes it to all of
these Google apps within days and can make it to millions
of users within a week. Now, that also means
that for new features, we are adding feature
flags to make sure that we allow for early testing
by these Google apps or by you in alpha versions of Compose. And you can test
them, and you can let us know if there are any
performance regressions or bugs with the implementation. And so we have this quick
iteration of testing out new functionality. And at some point, whenever
the changes make it into a beta or a stable
version of Compose, they've often been running, in
production, in these Google apps with millions of users
for weeks, if not months. That should give you
a lot more certainty in upgrading to the latest
Compose stable version whenever it comes out. Now, another stability
improvement that we did is that we did a big sweep of
all of our experimental APIs. You shared with us
that having too many of these experimental
APIs can sometimes make the API surface feel
a little bit less stable. So we went through them. And for many of the
APIs, we cleaned them up and made sure that we
could stabilize them. But some of them were actually
experimental for several years now, because we
eventually wanted to get around to fixing them. But they're either good
enough as they are, and we stabilize them now, or
we deprecated them and we will remove them for now. So with that, we reduced
32% of the experimental APIs over the course
of just one year.

end-to-end ML model that takes in input from

the huge potential of large multi-modal language
models for autonomous driving. So to leverage that
potential, we've been developing what we call the
Waymo Foundation Model, which combines the AV domain expertise
of the Waymo AI backbone and the general world
knowledge of VLMs. It's essentially an
encoder and decoder model. The job of the encoder, or the
perception side on the left, is to take the data from
all of our sensors-- the LiDARs, the
cameras, the radars-- and to compress it or encode
it into a representation that contains all of the
information that's relevant for the
task of driving. And the decoder, the
part on the right, that's the generative part. That's the part that understands
the social interactive aspects of driving that
can model behavior. It can predict what
other people will do. It can generate trajectories
and driving behaviors. It's also the foundation
for generative models of agent behavior that
we use for simulation. And that brings me to the second
key challenge of autonomous driving, just beyond the
building of a good driver, and that's evaluation. Now, key to good evaluation
is a good simulator. So what makes a good simulator? Well, first of
all, it needs to be able to run at very large scale,
simulating billions of miles. Second, it needs to be
able to run in closed loop, meaning that if you take an
action in this simulated world, the world responds
appropriately. And third, it needs
to be realistic, both from the perspective
of sensor realism as well as from the perspective
of behavioral realism, meaning that all of the
simulated agents, all the simulated road users,
are behaving the way they would in real life. So we have a bit of an
interesting chicken and egg

be ignored and they'll fall back to their 2D counterparts. Orbiters are floating elements
that typically contain controls the user can interact with. Orbiters are anchored to spatial
panels or other entities, such as spatial layouts. Orbiters can help
declutter your app's UI and bring the user's attention
to important controls, such as a navigation rail,
like you see in this example. By wrapping the navigation
rail with an orbiter, it extracts it from
your 2D layout. From here, you'd
specify where and how you'd like to attach
it to your app's panel. Adding elevation to
spatial UI components can help get a user's attention,
creates better hierarchy, and can improve legibility. Spatial dialogues
and spatial popups are two types of
spatialized components that can help add
depth and elevation to your app in meaningful ways. Spatial dialogues provide a way
to present important information or prompts to the user in a
focused and modal manner within the 3D environment. When spatialization is
enabled, the dialogue will be placed higher in Z-depth
to display an elevated dialogue and scrim panels. Spatial popups behave similarly. And when you want even
more fine-grained control over elevation in your
UI, spatial elevation allows you to elevate any
composable in your app to a higher Z-depth
than the spatial panel. Up until now, we've
been looking at how you can build and
customize spatial UI and content for your apps
using Jetpack Compose for XR. In addition, material design
provides components and layouts that adapt for XR. You can adapt your current
material three implementation by adding the
EnableXrComponentOverrides wrapper. You can use this
wrapper with components such as navigation
rail or navigation bar, and those components
will automatically adapt to an orbiter if
the app is in Full Space.

by adding the

artwork example app. Using this wrapper results

of XR headsets, where each eye has
its own display, this form factor is
particularly well suited for viewing
stereoscopic content. Stereoscopic video,
sometimes called 3D video, creates the illusion of 3D depth
by showing slightly different images or streams of
content to each eye. Much like what happens
when we view things in the real world with
our own eyes, these two different images, or
streams of content, are combined by our brain
to produce that single image with the perception of depth. Each image in a
stereoscopic video is encoded as two eyes,
either in a top/bottom format, as shown here, or in
a side by side format. We're excited to introduce
SpatialExternalSurface, a new composable
that can support rendering both stereoscopic and
monoscopic, or regular video formats. While some of the concepts

Each task model is made up

and modeling into one class, making it really easy to use. You can use tasks for either

classifier preprocessor. This takes raw data, whether
images, text, or audio, and converts it into a format
that the model can understand, like tensors. This pre-processing step is
built into high-level task class, but you can also use

query: What are the main parts of this ink library, and how do they help you create, display, and edit ink strokes?

reranked_docs.title: Google I/O 2025 – MmeJSLAnB-M
reranked_docs.text:
from generating stroke
shapes with stylus input, to rendering those strokes
quickly and efficiently, and even performing complex
geometric calculations for things like
erasing and selection. We built the ink Jetpack
library to simplify the process of creating,
rendering, and manipulating beautiful ink strokes. It provides a powerful
and modular toolkit for integrating rich inking
features into your app. The library consists
of six modules. The Strokes module is the
foundation of the ink library, handling core data types for
interacting with ink input. The Authoring module
manages the capture of user input and real
time rendering of strokes and facilitates
low-latency rendering. The Brush module manages
the visual style of strokes through properties like
color, size, and texture. The Geometry module provides
geometric primitives for shape manipulation
and enables features like stroke erasing
and selection. The Rendering
module incorporates canvasStrokeRenderer and
viewStrokeRenderer, offering performant rendering pipelines
with anti-aliasing support for Compose and
view-based UIs. Finally, the Storage module
provides efficient storage of stroke input data. As a developer, you can
use only the modules needed for your app's architecture. We've received great feedback
from our early partners. Ink API significantly reduced
the workload required for them to implement responsive
fluid inking in their app. Now let's take a look
at the Brush module and some of the
recent improvements we made with custom brushes. You can use the Brush module
in its factory methods to get started quickly
using stock brushes for pressure-sensitive
pins, highlighters, markers, and dashed lines. Here you see how I quickly
created a brush with the stock pressure pin and set the color,
size, and epsilon of the brush. We now have added support
for customized brushes with new experimental
APIs, which lets you unleash your
creativity to implement fun, expressive brushes,
or just match your existing set of brushes.

introduced Project Mariner
at the end of last year. It is a research prototype

improve our Chrome extensions documentation by adding
more code samples. Go to the Chrome extensions
reference documentation and create a list of all
Chrome extension APIs that don't have a code example. Watch how the agent will
come up with a plan on how to approach this problem. It uses Google Search to find

side panel API to display what the
agent is currently doing. When performing
a task, the agent needs to know which actions

screenshot of the page and uses the DOM to identify

link to the Alarms API page would be such a possible action. The screenshot with
the annotated actions is then sent to a
server that uses Gemini to decide what the

a button and explain why it thinks that this
is the next best step. The server then
sends the next action to perform, together
with the explanation why back to the
Chrome extensions. The Chrome extension then
performs the action on the page, observes the new state, and
sends us back to the server to ask what it should do next. And this goes on until
the task is completed. One interesting question
from an extension developer

reranked_docs.text: but it generates a structured output saying which tool it wants to use and what information to pass to it. This tool calling capability leads to this second main paradigm, the agent. Instead of a fixed chain here, we put the LLM in the driver's seat, running it in a loop. The LLM gets a task, calls a tool, feeds the result back to the LM, and lets it decide the next step. It continues this loop until it determines the task is complete. The big advantage here is flexibility. The agent is not locked into a predefined sequence. However, this flexibility often leads to lower reliability. These basic agent where the LLM is making all the decisions in a loop can often go off track. And here's why. Maybe the instructions given to the LLM were ambiguous. Maybe the tools available weren't quite right or their descriptions were misleading. Perhaps the LLM failed to reason correctly about the steps needed and tries to rely on its internal knowledge instead of using a tool. So while powerful, this simple looping approach can be unreliable in practice. So this brings us to the core challenge. On one hand, we have change reliable, predictable but rigid and on the other hand we have these agents flexible and dynamic but unreliable. So the question is can we have both? Can we build systems that have the reliability of structured workflows and also the flexibility of agents? One way to achieve this is to model your control flow with a graph like a state machine. In this graph, you have nodes representing specific decision points. Some might execute code, some might call an LLM, some might call a tool, and then you have edges that define how you transition between these nodes representing the logic and flow of the application. So this is the approach that langraph uses. Lang graph is a framework designed specifically to build these kinds of graph-based AI applications. This core ID is to let you explicitly define parts of the control flow using code giving you the reliability of chains when you need it

In the last year, we released

with the seq and seq2 types. In classic Go fashion,
iterators are more than just a new type in the
standard library. They're an elegant
way to simplify loops using the already familiar
for range expression and keeping the mechanics
of iterating separate from the body of your loop. Before iterators, there were
a couple of different ways to loop over data. Some methods would
return a slice containing all the results, which
can be inefficient for large collections. Another way was to create
your own iterator object, like this code, which uses the
Google Cloud Storage library. Notice the complexity here. We have flow control and
error checking in our loop. And that error checking needs
to be duplicated in every loop. With iterators, you can
use the familiar for range syntax to perform the loop. And the complex flow control
is kept inside the iterator. This lets our loop
body stay focused on the handling of our
files or our errors without worrying
about flow control. The standard library
as of 1.24 includes a bunch of iterators
in the strings, slices, and maps packages. Because iterators

for the Cloud Storage example. The declaration

query: Hey, what free/open-source tools do people use to fine-tune models built on Gemma, and what are some notable multilingual projects that run on Gemma (like a Korean version, GemmaX, SILMA Kashif)?

reranked_docs.title: Google I/O 2025 – o7Bv4r08FBM
reranked_docs.text:
are two other very
popular opensource libraries to fine-tune models. I will not dive too much into
the code for the sake of time. But with Transformers,
for example, there is trl, which is a
high-level wrapper that allows you to very easily,
just with a SFTTrainer, do the training of a model. Or Unsloth. Unsloth is a wrapper around
Hugging Face Transformers that makes it very, very, very
easy to fine-tune models with very limited compute. So in a free Google Colab,
in a Kaggle instance, for example, you can use
Unsloth to train models. And this is extremely quickly,
without losing any quality. So it's a very strong
technique to do finetuning. And there are over 70,000
Gemma-based models built by the community. The top three are
multilingual examples. So we have seen people training
Gemma for Korean conversations. We have seen GemmaX, which
is Gemma for translation. We have seen SILMA Kashif,
which is a retrieval augmented generation for
Arabic and English. But we have also
seen people using Gemma to change the way
they develop models, that they build models. So Lumina Image
is a way in which they combine Gemma
with a few other models to do image generation. Or OmniAudio, which
is a model that can also take audio as input. Or finally, SimPO, which is a
different post-train technique. It's a different RLHF
technique based on Gemma. So we are seeing people
building research, also, on top of the open
models that we release. It's been fantastic to see
what the community built. Just a couple of months ago, we
did a Kaggle competition, focused on enabling
the community to train Gemma for their own languages. And we saw thousands of people
participating, contributing, sharing knowledge,
collaborating. So this was very exciting. And now I'm happy
to hand it over again to Gus, who will
talk a bit more about some of his favorite case studies. Thanks. [APPLAUSE] GUS MARTINS: Thank you. Nice. Yeah, you thought you didn't
have me anymore, right? So let's talk about how
people are leveraging Gemma even further.

bits for every single parameter. So kind of rough napkin

can I actually use these models? So there is a family
of techniques called "quantization." Quantization reduces the
precision of the parameters. So you can go down to 8 bits per
parameter, 4 bits, even 2 bits.
